items.title: Definition and exclusion of alcohol
items.contentHtml:
Definition and exclusion of alcohol

Nonalcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) is defined by the presence of hepatic steatosis (≥5% hepatocytes) in the absence of secondary causes of fat accumulation. Alcohol exclusion requires <20 g/day in women and <30 g/day in men (or <140 g/week in women and <210 g/week in men). It is mandatory to rule out viral hepatitis, steatogenic drugs (amiodarone, methotrexate, tamoxifen) and hereditary metabolic diseases. In the context of ENARM/MIR, the catch is that a patient with an intake of 40 g/day of alcohol does not meet the criteria and is classified as ALD (alcoholic liver disease).

items.title: Spectrum: steatosis to steatohepatitis
items.contentHtml:
Spectrum: steatosis to steatohepatitis

NAFLD ranges from simple steatosis (fat without inflammation or hepatocyte injury) to non-alcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH), which requires lobular inflammation and hepatocellular ballooning with or without fibrosis. Progression to cirrhosis occurs only in NASH. In biopsy, NAS (NAFLD Activity Score) ≥5 defines NASH; <3 discards. The presence of fibrosis (stage F2-F4) is the main predictor of mortality. In tests: they ask that simple steatosis does not raise transaminases significantly or progress to cirrhosis, while NASH does.

items.title: Ultrasound criteria
items.contentHtml:
Diagnostic imaging criteria (ultrasound)

Abdominal ultrasound is the first line of diagnosis. Criteria: increased hepatic echogenicity with respect to renal parenchyma, attenuation of the deep ultrasound beam and blurring of the intrahepatic vessels. Sensitivity is >85% if steatosis >20-30%. It does not differentiate simple steatosis from NASH nor does it detect fibrosis. The catch: ultrasound can be normal if steatosis <20%, but the gold standard is still biopsy. In ENARM/MIR: the combination of ultrasound + elevated transaminases does not confirm NASH, it only suggests.

items.title: Liver biopsy
items.contentHtml:
Liver biopsy as the gold standard

The liver biopsy is the gold standard for diagnosing NASH and staging fibrosis (stages F0-F4). Indicates: suspicion of NASH with significant fibrosis (F≥2) or diagnostic doubts. Evaluates steatosis (>5%), hepatocellular ballooning, lobular inflammation and fibrosis. The NAS (NAFLD Activity Score) adds these three components (0-8). Fibrosis is staged according to CRN (Clinical Research Network): F0 without fibrosis, F1 perisinusoidal, F2 periportal, F3 bridges, F4 cirrhosis. Trap: biopsy is not indicated in all patients with steatosis; only if there is a high risk of NASH/fibrosis.

items.title: NFS index
items.contentHtml:
NAFLD Fibrosis Score (NFS)

The NAFLD Fibrosis Score (NFS) is a non-invasive scale that estimates advanced fibrosis (F3-F4). Formula: -1.675 + 0.037 × age (years) + 0.094 × BMI (kg/m²) + 1.13 × glucose alteration/diabetes (yes=1, no=0) + 0.99 × AST/ALT – 0.013 × platelets (×10⁹/L) – 0.66 × albumin (g/dL). Cutoff points: <-1.455 rules out advanced fibrosis (NPV >93%); >0.676 predicts advanced fibrosis (PPV >82%); Intermediate values ​​require elastography or biopsy. In examinations: it is the most used tool to stratify fibrosis risk in NAFLD.

items.title: metabolic syndrome
items.contentHtml:
Association with metabolic syndrome

NAFLD is considered the hepatic manifestation of metabolic syndrome. More than 80% of patients with NAFLD have obesity (BMI >30) and/or type 2 diabetes. Metabolic syndrome criteria (ATP III): abdominal circumference >102 cm (men) or >88 cm (women), triglycerides ≥150 mg/dL, HDL <40/<50 mg/dL, BP ≥130/85 mmHg, fasting glucose ≥100 mg/dL. Each component is independently associated with increased risk of NASH and fibrosis. Catch: Weight loss improves all components and steatosis, but does not reverse advanced fibrosis.

items.title: Elevation of transaminases
items.contentHtml:
Elevated transaminases (AST/ALT)

In NAFLD, transaminases are usually mild to moderately elevated (1.5-4 times the upper limit of normal). ALT predominates over AST in the initial stages (simple steatosis). Elevation does not correlate well with the degree of fibrosis; Patients with advanced NASH may have normal transaminases. In ENARM/MIR: normalization of transaminases does not imply resolution of NASH. Causes of elevation: oxidative stress, lipotoxicity and enzyme release by damaged hepatocytes. Persistent elevation >6 months with ultrasound steatosis suggests NAFLD/NASH.

items.title: AST/ALT ratio <1
items.contentHtml:
AST/ALT <1 ratio in NAFLD

In NAFLD, the AST/ALT <1 ratio is characteristic (ALT greater than AST), because ALT is more specific to the hepatocyte and is released in a greater proportion. As fibrosis progresses to cirrhosis, the relationship may reverse (>1). In alcoholic hepatitis, the ratio is usually >1.5-2 (AST > ALT). The catch: in advanced NASH with F3-F4 fibrosis, the AST/ALT ratio can be >1, simulating alcoholic disease. Therefore, a ratio <1 supports NAFLD, but does not rule out significant fibrosis. In exams: they ask that a ratio >2 suggests alcoholic liver disease.

items.title: Weight loss and exercise
items.contentHtml:
Treatment: weight loss and exercise

Weight loss ≥7-10% of body weight is the first-line treatment for NAFLD/NASH. Reduces steatosis, inflammation and, if ≥10%, can reverse fibrosis in some cases. The recommended diet is hypocaloric (deficit of 500-1000 kcal/day) with restriction of refined carbohydrates and saturated fats. Aerobic exercise (≥150 min/week) and resistance exercise improve insulin sensitivity independently of weight loss. There is no drug approved specifically for NAFLD; Weight loss is the most effective intervention. Trap: losses <5% do not improve steatohepatitis.

items.title: Vitamin E in NASH
items.contentHtml:
Vitamin E in steatohepatitis

Vitamin E (tocopherol) at a dose of 800 IU/day is indicated in patients with biopsy-proven NASH and without type 2 diabetes (PIVENS trial). It reduces steatosis, inflammation and ballooning, but does not improve fibrosis. Contraindicated in diabetes due to increased cardiovascular risk and in decompensated cirrhosis. It is not used in simple steatosis. In ENARM/MIR: they ask that vitamin E is the first pharmacological line in non-diabetic NASH, but not in diabetics or cirrhotics. Its use is off-label in many countries, but supported by AASLD/EASL guides.

items.title: Pioglitazone in DM2
items.contentHtml:
Pioglitazone in type 2 diabetes

Pioglitazone (30 mg/day) is a PPAR-γ agonist indicated in patients with proven NASH and type 2 diabetes or prediabetes (PIVENS+ trial). It improves steatosis, inflammation and ballooning, and may reduce fibrosis in some studies. Adverse effects: weight gain (2-4 kg), edema and risk of fractures in women. It is not used in patients without diabetes due to its safety profile. Contraindicated in heart failure (NYHA III-IV). In exams: they ask that pioglitazone is the pharmacological option in NASH with DM2, while vitamin E is reserved for non-diabetics.

items.foco: Risk of cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma
items.title: Risk of cirrhosis and HCC
items.contentHtml:
Risk of cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma

NAFLD is the fastest growing cause of cirrhosis globally. The risk of progression to cirrhosis is 15-25% in NASH in 10-15 years. Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) can develop even in the absence of cirrhosis (up to 30% of HCC cases due to NAFLD). Risk factors: F3-F4 fibrosis, type 2 diabetes, obesity, age >50 years, male sex. Screening for HCC with ultrasound every 6 months is recommended in NAFLD cirrhosis. Pitfall: HCC can occur in NASH without cirrhosis, so surveillance should be considered in patients with advanced fibrosis (F3).
